explanation: The Sigma rule specifically monitors 'regedit.exe' (selection_img). By using 'reg.exe', which is the command-line alternative for registry manipulation, the rule is completely bypassed while achieving the same result of importing a registry key from an Alternate Data Stream (ADS).

The rule relies on the process name ending in '\regedit.exe' or the OriginalFileName metadata. By copying the binary to a different filename, the 'Image|endswith' check fails. Additionally, using the '/s' (silent) switch instead of '/i' (import) avoids one of the specific command-line strings the rule looks for, although the regex for ADS might still trigger if the filename isn't changed.

The rule triggers on the presence of ' /i ' or '.reg' in the command line. By using the undocumented or less common '-m' switch (which allows multiple instances but often triggers imports in specific contexts) or simply avoiding the '/i' flag entirely, you can evade the 'selection_cli' logic. Note that regedit performs an import by default on many systems when a file is passed as an argument even without '/i'.

This bypass technique extracts the content of the Alternate Data Stream to a temporary standard file first. When regedit.exe is then called, the CommandLine no longer contains the colon (':') required by the regular expression ':[^ \\]', which is the rule's primary method for detecting ADS usage.
